name: Science
description: Physics, chemistry, biology, anatomy, and the solar system

questions.q: Which scientist formulated the three laws of motion, including 'for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction'?

source.notes: Published in Principia Mathematica, 1687.

questions.q: On a lever, what is the fixed pivot point called?

source.notes: Archimedes: 'Give me a lever long enough and a fulcrum on which to place it, and I shall move the world.'

questions.q: Which temperature scale begins at absolute zero (−273.15 °C)?

source.notes: Named after Lord Kelvin. 0 K = no molecular motion.

questions.q: The Beaufort Scale is used to measure the intensity of what?

source.notes: Created 1805 by Sir Francis Beaufort. Ranges from 0 (calm) to 12 (hurricane).

questions.q: On the periodic table, the symbol 'Na' represents which element?

source.notes: From Latin 'natrium.' Atomic number 11.

questions.q: Which metallic element, also known as 'quicksilver,' is liquid at room temperature?

source.notes: Symbol Hg from Latin 'hydrargyrum.' Bromine is also liquid but is a nonmetal.

questions.q: What is the addictive chemical compound found in tobacco?

source.notes: Named after Jean Nicot, who introduced tobacco to France in 1560.

questions.q: What protein makes up human fingernails and hair?

source.notes: Also found in animal hooves, horns, and feathers.

questions.q: In what year did astronauts last walk on the Moon?

source.notes: Apollo 17, December 1972. Gene Cernan was the last to walk on the surface.

questions.q: Which animal has the longest gestation period of any land mammal, at roughly 22 months?

source.notes: African elephant ~22 months, Asian ~18-22 months.

questions.q: A Geiger counter is a device used to detect what?

source.notes: Invented by Hans Geiger in 1908.

questions.q: How many officially recognized constellations are there in the night sky?

source.notes: Standardized by the IAU in 1922. 48 from Ptolemy's original list.

questions.q: Approximately how many Earth years does it take Jupiter to orbit the Sun?

source.notes: Actually 11.86 years. Saturn takes ~29 years.

questions.q: Which space probe, launched in 1997, orbited Saturn and studied its rings and moons for 13 years?

source.notes: Arrived at Saturn 2004. Huygens lander touched down on Titan. Mission ended 2017.

questions.q: What was the name of the first artificial satellite, launched by the Soviet Union in 1957?

source.notes: Launched October 4, 1957. Orbited Earth for 3 months.

questions.q: Who performed the first spacewalk (EVA) in 1965?

source.notes: March 18, 1965. Voskhod 2 mission. Ed White was the first American, June 1965.

questions.q: Occam's Razor is a problem-solving principle that favors what kind of explanation?

source.notes: Named after William of Ockham (~1287-1347). 'Entities should not be multiplied beyond necessity.'

questions.q: In physics, force equals mass times what?

source.notes: Newton's second law: F = ma.

questions.q: How many pairs of chromosomes does a typical human cell contain?

source.notes: 23 pairs = 46 total. Sex chromosomes are the 23rd pair.

questions.q: What is the tallest known volcano in the solar system, located on Mars?

source.notes: ~21.9 km tall, ~600 km in diameter. Shield volcano.

questions.q: Which planet is named after the Roman god of the sea?

source.notes: Discovered 1846 by Johann Galle. 8th planet from the Sun.

questions.q: What is the outermost layer of human skin called?

source.notes: Composed mainly of keratinocytes. No blood vessels.
